content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size 6 min read 2 hours ago -- Introduction In the previous article, we learned how to configure Redux Toolkit Query (RTK Query) and perform GET requests using builder.query(). We fetched data from an API and displayed it in a React application without writing useEffect(), useState(), or manual API logic. However, fetching data is only one part of working with APIs. Real-world applications also need to create, update, and delete data. For example: • A registration page sends new user information to the server. • A login page sends credentials for authentication. • A blog application creates new posts. • An e-commerce website creates new orders. • A task management application adds new tasks. All of these operations involve sending data from the frontend to the backend, which is typically done using an HTTP POST request. In RTK Query, POST requests are handled using Mutations, not Queries. In this article, we’ll learn how to send data to a server using builder.mutation(). Every line of code, every function, and every configuration option will be explained in detail so you understand not only how it works, but also why it works. Understanding HTTP Methods Before writing any code, it’s important to understand why we use different HTTP methods. A REST API usually supports several request types: MethodPurposeExampleGETRetrieve dataGet all usersPOSTCreate new dataAdd a new userPUTReplace existing dataReplace a userPATCHUpdate part of existing dataUpdate user nameDELETERemove dataDelete a user In this article, our focus is POST, which is used to create new resources on the server. Why Doesn’t POST Use builder.query()? Many beginners ask this question. If builder.query() fetches data, why can't it also send data? The answer lies in their purpose. Queries Queries are designed for reading data. Examples include: • Get Users • Get Products • Get Orders • Get Posts Queries automatically cache responses because the same data may be requested multiple times. Mutations Mutations are designed for changing data. Examples include: • Create User • Update User • Delete User • Login • Register Mutations tell the server that something has changed. Because of this, RTK Query treats them differently from queries. What We Are Going to Build We’ll create a simple form that sends a new user to the server. The API endpoint will be: https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/usersThe request body will look like this: { "name": "John Doe", "email": "john@example.com" }Project Structure src │ ├── app │ └── store.js │ ├── services │ └── api.js │ ├── components │ └── AddUser.jsx │ ├── App.jsx │ └── main.jsxOur Redux Store setup remains the same. We only need to add a Mutation Endpoint and create a component that submits data. Step 1 — Create a Mutation Endpoint Open: src/services/api.jsAdd the following endpoint inside the endpoints section. import { createApi, fetchBaseQuery } from "@reduxjs/toolkit/query/react";export const api = createApi({ reducerPath: "api", baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({ baseUrl: "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/", }), endpoints: (builder) => ({ addUser: builder.mutation({ query: (newUser) => ({ url: "users", method: "POST", body: newUser, }), }), }),});export const { useAddUserMutation, } = api;Let’s understand every line. Understanding builder.mutation() addUser: builder.mutation({Unlike builder.query(), which retrieves data, builder.mutation() is used whenever we want to modify server data. Typical use cases include: • Creating users • Registering accounts • Logging in • Updating products • Deleting posts Whenever your application changes backend data, use a Mutation. Understanding query() query: (newUser) => ({The function receives whatever data we pass from React. Suppose we dispatch: addUser({ name: "John", email: "john@example.com", });The parameter: newUsercontains: { name: "John", email: "john@example.com" }This object becomes the request body. Understanding URL url: "users",Since our base URL is: https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/RTK Query automatically creates: https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/usersNo need to repeat the entire URL. Understanding Method method: "POST",This tells RTK Query to send a POST request. Without specifying the method, the request defaults to GET. Understanding Body body: newUser,Everything inside newUser is sent to the server. Example request: { "name": "John", "email": "john@example.com" }The server receives this object exactly as it appears. Step 2 — Export the Generated Hook export const { useAddUserMutation, } = api;Just like Queries automatically generated: useGetUsersQuery()Mutations automatically generate: useAddUserMutation()We didn’t write this Hook manually. RTK Query generated it for us. Step 3 — Create the React Component Create: src/components/AddUser.jsxAdd the following code. import { useState } from "react";import { useAddUserMutation } from "../services/api";const AddUser = () => { const [name, setName] = useState(""); const [email, setEmail] = useState(""); const [ addUser, { isLoading, isSuccess, error, }, ] = useAddUserMutation(); const handleSubmit = async (e) => { e.preventDefault(); await addUser({ name, email, }); setName(""); setEmail(""); }; return (
setName(e.target.value)} /> setEmail(e.target.value)} /> {isLoading &&

Saving...

} {isSuccess &&

User Added Successfully.

} {error &&

Something went wrong.

}
);};export default AddUser;Now let’s understand everything. Understanding useAddUserMutation() const [ addUser, { isLoading, isSuccess, error, }, ] = useAddUserMutation();Unlike Queries, Mutation Hooks return an array, not an object. The first item: addUseris the function that sends the request. The second item contains useful information about the request. Understanding addUser() await addUser({ name, email, });This sends the following request: POST /userswith this JSON body: { "name": "John", "email": "john@example.com" }The server receives this object and creates a new user. Understanding Mutation States RTK Query automatically provides several useful states. isLoading isLoadingBecomes true while the request is being sent. Useful for disabling buttons or showing loaders. isSuccess isSuccessBecomes true after the request succeeds. Useful for displaying success messages or redirecting users. error errorContains server errors if something goes wrong. Useful for displaying friendly error messages. Step 4 — Render the Component Open: src/App.jsxReplace everything with: import AddUser from "./components/AddUser";function App() { return ; }export default App;Now run the project. npm run devFill out the form and click Add User. RTK Query sends the POST request automatically. Complete Request Flow Here’s what happens behind the scenes. User Fills Form │ ▼ Clicks Submit │ ▼ addUser() │ ▼ Generated Mutation Hook │ ▼ RTK Query │ ▼ fetchBaseQuery() │ ▼ POST Request │ ▼ Server Response │ ▼ Mutation State Updates │ ▼ React Re-rendersNotice how we never wrote: • fetch() • axios.post() • useEffect() • Manual loading state • Manual error state RTK Query handled all of that for us. builder.query() vs builder.mutation() Understanding the difference between these two methods is essential. Featurebuilder.query()builder.mutation()PurposeFetch dataModify dataTypical HTTP MethodsGETPOST, PUT, PATCH, DELETEGenerates HookuseGetUsersQuery()useAddUserMutation()Automatic Fetch on RenderYesNoTriggered ManuallyNoYesCommon Use CasesReading dataCreating, updating, deleting data Choosing the correct one keeps your API layer predictable and easy to understand. Best Practices When working with POST requests in RTK Query, keep these recommendations in mind: • Use builder.mutation() for any operation that changes server data. • Keep request payloads small and send only the fields the API expects. • Always handle isLoading, isSuccess, and error to improve the user experience. • Give endpoints descriptive names such as addUser, createPost, or registerUser. • Validate form input before sending data to the server. • Consider using unwrap() if you want to handle success and errors with try...catch in your components. Key Takeaways By completing this guide, you’ve learned how to: • Create a Mutation using builder.mutation(). • Configure a POST endpoint inside an API Slice. • Send JSON data to a backend server. • Use the automatically generated useAddUserMutation() Hook. • Trigger a POST request from a React form. • Handle loading, success, and error states without writing boilerplate. • Understand the difference between Queries and Mutations. • Follow best practices for creating clean, maintainable API interactions. This pattern is used in real-world applications for user registration, authentication, creating blog posts, submitting orders, and many other forms of data creation. What’s Next? Now that you’ve learned how to create data with POST requests, the next step is to update and remove existing data. In the next article, we’ll explore: • Updating data using PUT and PATCH requests. • Deleting records using DELETE requests. • Passing dynamic IDs to mutation endpoints. • Invalidating cache so UI updates automatically. • Using Tags (providesTags and invalidatesTags) to keep data synchronized without manually refetching. By the end of the next guide, you’ll be able to build a complete CRUD application with RTK Query using production-ready patterns.
